skill.name: routeros-mndp
skill.description: MNDP (MikroTik Neighbor Discovery Protocol) wire format, behavior, and RouterOS /ip/neighbor integration. Use when: implementing MNDP discovery, parsing MNDP packets, working with /ip/neighbor, understanding WinBox device discovery, debugging why a router doesn't appear in neighbor lists, or when the user mentions MNDP, neighbor discovery, WinBox discovery, or /ip/neighbor.
skill.summary: Technical reference for MNDP protocol wire format, TLV parsing, and RouterOS neighbor discovery integration
skill.author: tikoci

security_audit.summary: All 56 static findings are false positives. The skill contains only protocol documentation with code examples in markdown. No executable code, no external command execution, no credential exfiltration, and no malicious patterns detected. The detected external_commands and network patterns are markdown code blocks containing legitimate protocol documentation (broadcast addresses, socket examples, curl commands). This is a pure reference documentation skill for MikroTik MNDP protocol.

medium_findings.title: External Command Pattern False Positives
medium_findings.description: Static analyzer flagged 44 locations as 'Ruby/shell backtick execution'. These are all false positives - the file contains only markdown documentation with code blocks showing shell commands like curl, git, setsockopt, and hex values. No actual backtick execution exists in this file.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: Code review confirms all backtick occurrences are markdown formatting or documentation examples, not executable code.

medium_findings.title: Network Pattern False Positives
medium_findings.description: Static analyzer flagged broadcast address 255.255.255.255 and socket binding 0.0.0.0 as 'hardcoded network addresses'. These are standard protocol documentation for MNDP. The URL at line 151 uses a template placeholder <router-ip>, not a real endpoint.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: All network references are standard protocol documentation with no external connections or credential exposure.

medium_findings.title: Weak Cryptographic Algorithm False Positives
medium_findings.description: Static analyzer flagged hex values like 0x0000 as 'weak cryptographic algorithms'. These are TLV (Type-Length-Value) protocol field type identifiers, not cryptographic code.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: Hex values in this file represent protocol field types, not cryptographic algorithms.

medium_findings.title: System Reconnaissance False Positives
medium_findings.description: Static analyzer flagged legitimate protocol descriptions as 'system reconnaissance'. The file documents how MNDP protocol works, including multi-interface behavior and timing characteristics. This is expected documentation for a network protocol skill.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: Protocol documentation describing standard network discovery behavior, not malicious reconnaissance.

content.value_statement: Network engineers need to understand MNDP protocol format to build custom discovery tools, but protocol specifications are scattered across technical documents. This skill provides complete MNDP wire format documentation, TLV parsing examples, and RouterOS integration patterns.
actual_capabilities: Parse MNDP packet structure with TLV fields (MAC, Identity, Version, Platform, Board, Uptime, IP addresses)
Explain MNDP wire format including refresh packet (9-byte), response packet structure, and byte ordering
Implement UDP socket code for MNDP discovery in TypeScript and C with proper broadcast/multicast configuration
Integrate with RouterOS /ip/neighbor CLI commands and REST API endpoints
Handle multi-interface RouterOS devices that send separate MNDP announcements per interface
Apply security hardening by disabling MNDP on untrusted interfaces
limitations: Does not execute actual network discovery or communicate with RouterOS devices
Does not provide implementation in languages other than TypeScript and C
Does not include authentication or credential handling for RouterOS access

use_cases.title: Build Custom MNDP Scanner
use_cases.description: Use the wire format specification and TypeScript parsing example to build a custom MNDP discovery tool that identifies MikroTik devices on a network.

use_cases.title: Integrate with RouterOS Management
use_cases.description: Query RouterOS /ip/neighbor via REST API or CLI to get current neighbor list for network documentation or monitoring systems.

use_cases.title: Troubleshoot Discovery Issues
use_cases.description: Debug why a MikroTik router does not appear in neighbor lists by understanding timing, multi-interface behavior, and MNDP packet structure.

prompt_templates.title: Basic MNDP Query
prompt_templates.prompt: How do I parse an MNDP response packet? Show me the TLV format for extracting the identity and board name.
prompt_templates.scenario: When you need to understand MNDP packet structure

prompt_templates.title: Implement Discovery Socket
prompt_templates.prompt: Write a TypeScript function that sends an MNDP refresh packet and listens for responses on UDP port 5678.
prompt_templates.scenario: When you need to implement network discovery

prompt_templates.title: Query RouterOS Neighbors
prompt_templates.prompt: How do I get the list of discovered neighbors from RouterOS using the REST API?
prompt_templates.scenario: When you need to query RouterOS for neighbor information

prompt_templates.title: Security Hardening
prompt_templates.prompt: How do I disable MNDP on a specific interface in RouterOS to prevent device discovery from untrusted networks?
prompt_templates.scenario: When you need to secure RouterOS against neighbor discovery

output_examples.input: What does an MNDP response packet contain?
output_examples.output: An MNDP response contains: 2-byte type (0x0000), 2-byte sequence number, then TLV records. Common TLVs: type 1 (MAC address), type 5 (identity/hostname), type 7 (RouterOS version), type 8 (platform), type 10/13 (board name), type 11 (uptime in seconds), type 16 (interface name), type 17 (IPv4). Example: a RB4011 router returns identity 'MainOffice-Router', board 'RB4011iGS+5HacQ2HnD', version '7.18 (stable)'.

output_examples.input: Show me how to query neighbors via REST API
output_examples.output: Use curl to query RouterOS REST API: `curl -u admin:password http://<router-ip>/rest/ip/neighbor`. Response is a JSON array with fields: interface, address, mac-address, identity, platform, version, board. Each entry represents a discovered device on directly-connected networks.

output_examples.input: Why does my router appear multiple times in neighbor list?
output_examples.output: This is expected behavior. A RouterOS device sends separate MNDP announcements from each interface. If you have 4 active interfaces, you will see 4 entries with the same identity (hostname) but different MACs, IPs, and interface names. To deduplicate, group results by identity and use MAC address to distinguish devices with factory default name 'MikroTik'.

best_practices: Send multiple refresh packets during your listen window (every 5 seconds) to catch devices that miss the first broadcast due to packet loss or WiFi power-save
Group neighbor results by identity (hostname) to avoid displaying the same router multiple times when it has multiple interfaces
Treat MNDP information as sensitive - it reveals device identity, version, board model, IP addresses, MAC addresses, and uptime to anyone on the broadcast domain
anti_patterns: Do not assume all devices respond within 1 second - use a 10-30 second timeout depending on network type (WiFi, tunnels, high-latency links)
Do not interpret missing devices as offline - a short scan window produces partial results; increase timeout before concluding a device is unreachable
Do not ignore TLV type 13 for board name - some firmware versions use type 13 instead of type 10; check both

faq.question: What is the MNDP protocol?
faq.answer: MNDP (MikroTik Neighbor Discovery Protocol) is a UDP broadcast/multicast protocol on port 5678 that RouterOS uses for automatic device discovery. It is the same protocol that WinBox uses to populate its Neighbors tab.

faq.question: Does MNDP require authentication?
faq.answer: No. MNDP has no authentication - any device on the broadcast domain can send discovery packets and receive announcements. This is intentional for zero-configuration discovery but means the protocol reveals device information to anyone on the local network.

faq.question: What information does MNDP expose?
faq.answer: MNDP announcements reveal: device identity (hostname), RouterOS version, platform (usually MikroTik), board model, uptime, MAC address, IP address, and interface name. Treat this as sensitive information like an open SNMP community.

faq.question: How do I disable MNDP on RouterOS?
faq.answer: Use `/interface/list/member/add list=no-mndp interface=ether1` to exclude an interface, then `/ip/neighbor/discovery-settings/set discover-interface-list=!no-mndp` to apply the exclusion list.

faq.question: What is the difference between MNDP, CDP, and LLDP?
faq.answer: MNDP is MikroTik's proprietary protocol. CDP is Cisco's protocol. LLDP is the vendor-neutral IEEE 802.1AB standard. RouterOS supports all three via `/ip/neighbor/discovery-settings/set protocol=mndp,lldp,cdp`.

faq.question: Can MNDP cross routers?
faq.answer: No. MNDP is limited to the Layer 2 broadcast domain. It does not cross routers by default. For discovery across tunnels or overlays (like ZeroTier), expect longer response times of 5-20 seconds.
